event: residing at a long-term acute care hospital
suspected of having a urinary tract infection
urine specimen was collected 5 days prior to hospital admission
urine analysis was positive for large leukocyte esterase and bacteria
demonstrating resistance to ampicillin, levofloxacin, trimethoprim, and sulfamethoxazole
sensitivity to cefazolin, gentamicin, and nitrofurantoin
prescribed cephalexin 500 mg three times daily
started to develop an extensive erythematous rash
rash developed into sloughing of the skin
transferred to a local burn intensive care unit
developed pulmonary infiltrates with respiratory failure
diagnosed with toxic epidermal necrolysis syndrome (TENS)
estimated to cover approximately 56% of the total body surface area
involving his entire back, lower chest, abdomen, bilateral upper extremities, and bilateral thighs
white blood cell (WBC) count of 1.5 × 103/mm3 with 73% neutrophils
oxygen (O2) saturation was 99% on 6 L O2 via nasal cannula
pain was assessed at 9 within a 10-point pain scale
two peripheral blood cultures were obtained
skin surveillance cultures of nares, axilla, and groin, and a urine culture
from a newly placed indwelling Foley catheter
chest radiograph revealed right pleural effusion with right lower and left basilar opacities
placed on norepinephrine and vasopressin drips for pressure support
Lactated Ringer’s was given at 300 mL/h
titrated to the patient’s urine output
given single intravenous (IV) dose of gentamicin 120 mg
random serum gentamicin level (22.5 h post-dose) was 2.4 mg/dL
redosed with 120 mg of gentamicin 3 h later
supportive continuous renal replacement therapy was ordered
fluconazole continued at 200 mg IV every 24 h
urine culture showed >100,000 cfu/mL Candida species
physical examination performed on hospital day 5
sloughing of skin over his back, chest, upper extremities, abdomen, bilateral thighs, and buttocks
culture and sensitivity results of the tracheal aspirate grew Pseudomonas aeruginosa
sensitive to all anti-pseudomonal beta-lactams as well as gentamicin, tobramycin, amikacin, and colistin
but resistant to doripenem and quinolones
cause of death listed as cardiac arrest
multisystem organ failure due to TENS
autopsy reported TEN involving 80% of body surface area
Severity of Illness Score for TEN (SCORTEN) estimated a predicted mortality of 62%
